PageResearchNote.description:
LLM output for per-page comprehension during agentic search.

Each page read during the search loop is summarized into a research note
capturing key findings, perspectives covered, and knowledge contribution.

summary.description: 150-200 word research summary focused on findings relevant to query
summary.title: Summary

perspectives.description: STORM perspectives covered by this page
perspectives.title: Perspectives

key_facts.description: 3-5 specific facts/data points with numbers, dates, or named entities
key_facts.title: Key Facts

knowledge_contribution.description: What new understanding this page adds to the research
knowledge_contribution.title: Knowledge Contribution

PageResearchNote.title: PageResearchNote

schema_json.description: Batch wrapper for page research notes with invalid-note filtering.

notes.description: Research notes for each page read
notes.title: Notes

schema_json.title: PageSummaryBatch

prompt:
You are a research analyst reading web pages for a deep research project.

RESEARCH QUESTION: How does damp heat aging affect the adhesion strength of DVS-PEI encapsulants in flexible electronics?

Read the following 6 pages and create a research note for each.

--- PAGE: [PDF] Influence of different ageing conditions on the delamination ... ---
URL: https://pastel.hal.science/tel-05027404v1/file/2024UPSLM049_archivage.pdf
CONTENT:
HAL Id: tel-05027404 https://pastel.hal.science/tel-05027404v1 Submitted on 9 Apr 2025 HAL is a multi-disciplinary open access archive for the deposit and dissemination of scientific re- search documents, whether they are published or not. The documents may come from teaching and research institutions in France or abroad, or from public or pri- vate research centers. L’archive ouverte pluridisciplinaire HAL, est des- tinée au dépôt et à la diffusion de documents scien- tifiques de niveau recherche, publiés ou non, émanant des établissements d’enseignement et de recherche français ou étrangers, des laboratoires publics ou privés. HAL Authorization Influence of different ageing conditions on the delamination behaviour of photovoltaic modules Vincent Meslier To cite this version: Vincent Meslier. Influence of different ageing conditions on the delamination behaviour of photovoltaic mod- ules. Mechanics of materials [physics.class-ph]. Université Paris sciences et lettres, 2024. English. ⟨NNT : 2024UPSLM049⟩. ⟨tel-05027404⟩ Préparée à MINES Paris Influence of different ageing conditions on the delamination behaviour of photovoltaic modules Influence de différents vieillissements sur la délamination des modules photovoltaïques Soutenue par Vincent MESLIER Le 09 décembre 2024 Dirigée par Pierre-Olivier BOUCHARD École doctorale no364 Sciences Fondamentales et Appliquées Spécialité Mécanique Numérique et Matériaux Composition du jury : Étienne Barthel Directeur de recherche, ESPCI Paris Président Julie Diani Directrice de recherche, École Polytechnique Rapporteuse Bruno Fayolle Professeur, Arts et Métiers Paris Rapporteur Matthieu Despeisse Docteur, CSEM Examinateur Bertrand Chambion Docteur, CEA Grenoble Examinateur Jean-Luc Bouvard Professeur, Mines Paris PSL Examinateur Pierre-Olivier Bouchard Professeur, Mines Paris PSL Directeur de thèse Remerciements J’aimerais tout d’abord remercier chaleureusement mes trois encadrants. Bertrand, si mon stage de fin d’études ne s’était pas si bien déroulé, je ne me serais probablement pas engagé dans cette thèse, alors merci pour ta bienveillance, ta bonne humeur et ton regard critique. Jean-Luc, merci pour ton indulgence face à mes nombreux esclandres, à tes conseils avisés sur mon étude expérimentale et pour ton intransigeance sur la qualité de la rédaction. Pierre- Olivier, merci infiniment pour ta bienveillance, pour ta disponibilité ainsi que pour tous tes conseils, tant sur les simulations numériques que sur l’orientation professionnelle qui m’attend. Ce fut un réel plaisir de pouvoir travailler avec vous, de discuter science tout en s’envoyant des piques, d’avoir des encadrants avec de réelles qualités humaines et scientifiques. Vous m’avez fait grandir sur le plan professionnel mais aussi personnel et je vous en remercie grandement. Sur le plan professionnel, j’aimerais remercier spécialement Christophe Pradille pour toute l’aide que tu as pu m’apporter lors de mes expériences. Ton aide a été précieuse pour com- prendre et pouvoir utiliser rapidement la corrélation d’image. Sans ton aide, il m’aurait été impossible de prendre en main aussi rapidement le logiciel et ses subtilités. Ta bonne humeur a aussi été un rempart contre l’agacement face à ce logiciel capricieux. Je souhaiterais aussi remercier plus que chaleureusement Jean-Baptiste Charpentier pour tout le temps que tu as pu m’accorder, en te penchant sérieusement sur les problèmes que je te posais. J’ai beaucoup appris à ton contact. Merci infiniment pour ton aide sur la compréhen- sion des équations qu’utilise Abaqus, pour ton regard affûté sur mes résultats numériques et pour nos discussions sur l’expansion thermique et le comportement des polymères. Enfin, merci à Ichrak et Dylan pour les discussions qu’on a pu avoir ensemble. Vos travaux de thèse m’ont permis d’avoir une solide base de connaissance et des modèles numériques avancés. Les résultats que j’ai obtenus durant ces trois années ont été rendus possibles grâce à vous. Merci aussi à tous les collègues avec qui j’ai pu discuter, partager du temps pendant mes manips ou autour d’un café. Merci Marion, Timea, Marion, Margot, Eeva, Antoine, Philippe, Romain et Romain. J’aimerais aussi remercier tous mes amis avec qui j’ai pu partager des journées durant ces trois années, tant au CEMEF qu’à l’INES. Merci à Paul, Alan, Marion, Pablo, Aurèle, Léa, Franco, Maxime, Federico, Chloé, Caitline, Elie, Marion et Thibaut pour tous les bons moments passés au bureau, en rando ou à l’escalade. Merci à Lucie, Felicia, Antoine, Victor, Océane, Tristan, Louis, Shyrna et Hugo pour les soirées jeux, l’amour des gâteaux et les midis 2 au lac. Spéciale dédicace aux zikos avec qui j’ai pu faire de super chouettes concerts ! Merci Aurèle, Adrien, Franco, Laurianne, Léa, Rudy, Vincent, Victor, Paul, Florent et Hafsa ! Je ne me serai jamais autant amusé sans vous. Merci à mes amis de longue date. Merci Mélina pour notre belle amitié. Merci Déli, Neige, Sam, Guillaume, Pinok, Victor, Quv, Barre, Natasha et Léo pour tous nos concerts et les quatre cents coups qu’on a pu faire ensemble. Merci à ma famille, de m’avoir supporté dans mes choix de vie, qui m’ont parfois em- mené loin de vous. Loin des yeux près du cœur, dit l’adage, mais quand même, c’est dur parfois. Merci Maman de m’avoir transmis toutes tes valeurs. Merci Céline pour ta force de caractère qui nous a emmené de la confrontation à la confiance fraternelle. Merci Sacha pour nos discussions politico-écolo-sciento-humouristiques, ne t’inquiète pas, on va la changer cette société. Merci Colette et Philippe, pour tous les bons moments passés à Saint-Romain et nos nombreuses discussions et visites culturelles. Merci Florence, Olivier, Alice, Valentin et Romain. La personne que je suis aujourd’hui a été forgée grâce à votre contact à tous. Et enfin merci à ma famille d’adoption chambérienne qui m’a permis de passer de si bons moments, les week-ends et les soirs, même lors de la rédaction. Merci Camille pour les fous rires, les films de ta génération et pour la tarte à la myrtille. Merci Maë pour nos discussions philosophiques et sociologiques, pour ton regard extérieur et ta confiance. Si nos points de vue divergent sur certains points, les échanges que nous avons sont toujours enrichissants et de valeur pour moi. Merci Charlie pour ta chaleur et tes réconforts. Merci Nathan, oui les gens ne sont pas rationnels et sont égoïstes et c’est pour ça qu’on fonce dans le mur, mais non on ne peut pas supprimer l’humanité tout entière. Reste positif, tu fais déjà beaucoup à ton échelle. Et puis tu n’es pas seul, maintenant qu’on s’est trouvé, il ne nous reste plus qu’à appliquer le Programme. 3 Glossary Al-BSF Aluminium Back Surface Field ARC Anti-Reflective Coating CTE Coefficient --- PAGE: Emerging flexible photovoltaic technology: From materials to devices ---
URL: https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/ifm2.32
CONTENT:
Emerging flexible photovoltaic technology: From materials to devices Zhiyang Xu and Tangyue Xue contributed equally to this work. Abstract The third-generation photovoltaic technologies such as perovskite solar cells and organic solar cells, have low-temperature and solution-processing ability, flexibility and lightweight, which is expected to be developed into a wearable device that fits the human body. Here, the flexible substrates, transparent electrode materials, photovoltaic materials and devices for flexible solar cells are systematically introduced. First, the flexible substrates regarding their suitability are provided. Then, the research progress of commonly used transparent electrodes is reviewed, including poly(3,4-ethylenedioxythiophene):poly(styrene sulfonate) (PEDOT:PSS), metal nanowires, carbon-based materials, and composite electrode materials. Next, we summarize how to realize and optimize flexible devices, and how to balance the power conversion efficiency and mechanical stability of the device. Finally, we propose several promising research directions for intrinsically flexible solar cells. 1 INTRODUCTION In recent years, the concept of portable electronics has been realized in products such as flexible displays for cell phones, electronic sensing skins, and flexible solar cell modules. Portable electronic devices are attracting attention because of their lightweight, flexible, wearable, and smart compatibility and have shown far-reaching market potential. The development of portable electronic devices has also stimulated market demand for next-generation photovoltaic applications.1-4 Due to the flexibility of some third-generation photovoltaic materials, such as organic and perovskite materials, they are expected to be developed for use in nonplanar areas in terms of applications, manufacturing flexible photovoltaic devices, or even wearable devices that fit the human body.4-9 Lightweight, high energy-to-quality ratio of flexible devices will have a huge application space, which can provide more markets for economic development. Flexible photovoltaic devices have become the forefront of scientific research today. Since the glass substrate is removed from the flexible device, its lower quality and higher energy-mass ratio is significantly improved. Flexible devices have excellent resistance to bending, torsion, and stretching, as well as commercial advantages such as lightweight and portability.5, 7 For instance, Zhu et al. prepared perovskite solar cells (PSCs) with excellent power of 30.3 W·g−1 per weight.10 Therefore, flexible devices could be worn on the human body. Compatible with and matching the flexible expansion and contraction of human tissue, it can withstand more than 10% elastic deformation.6, 7 Given the rapid growth of this emerging field, we aim to provide a detailed overview of flexible photovoltaic device design and fabrication methods. Firstly, we discuss the flexible substrates for preparing flexible photovoltaic devices; there is a slight difference in the substrate used for the preparation of flexible photovoltaic devices. Secondly, we review transparent electrode materials directly related to photovoltaic performance and flexibility, focusing on polymer poly(3,4-ethylenedioxythiophene):poly(styrene sulfonate) (PEDOT:PSS), silver nanowires (Ag NWs), carbon nanomaterials, and composite electrode materials in the development of flexible photovoltaic devices. Thirdly, we summarize two photovoltaic materials, organic and perovskite, and explain why they were suitable to fabricate flexible photovoltaic devices. Afterward, we illustrate some recent reports on flexible photovoltaic devices made using different methods. Finally, a summary of major milestones in flexible photovoltaic devices and an outlook for further development is provided. 2 INTRINSICALLY FLEXIBLE SUBSTRATES The efficient and reliable operation of flexible photovoltaic device is inseparable from the flexible substrate, transparent electrode, and photovoltaic layer (Figure 1).8, 11-13 Emerging photovoltaic devices (perovskites and organic) are obtained generally by stacking layers one by one on a transparent electrode attached to glass. However, the glass substrate accounts for a large percentage of the mass of the entire device, power-per weight (the ratio of output power to the device weight) proposed as a vital metric for evaluating photovoltaic devices.10 Flexible substrate have become candidate for photovoltaic device by lightweight, flexibility, and roll-to-roll processability. 2.1 Flexible transparent substrates Polyethylene terephthalate (PET), polyethylene naphthalate (PEN), polyethersulfone (PES), and colorless polyimide (PI) are the most commonly used flexible transparent substrates for flexible solar cells.8, 11-13 There are a variety of flexible substrates with indium tin oxide (ITO) in the market, such as ITO/PEN, ITO/PET. Good solvent resistance and discrete temperature tolerance allow thin polymeric foils to maintain their intrinsic transparency when coated with ITO. Thin polymer foils are usually processed at temperatures below 120–180°C, which makes them particularly limited in terms of processing temperature and time. PEN, for example, has a temperature tolerance of 180°C and can be applied to devices with a processing temperature of 150°C. PET can only withstand heating to 120°C and is therefore more demanding in terms of processing temperature during device preparation, which can be used in devices prepared at low temperatures, such as inverted PSCs or organic solar cells (OSCs). In general, these thin polymer foils can only withstand continuous heating for not more than 30 min. Huang et al. reported flexible corning willow glass, a substrate with better temperature tolerance and flatness than PET and PEN substrate, but with limited bendable curvature radius.14 In addition, the thickness of the flexible substrate and ITO electrodes also influence the bendability of flexible solar cells. A thicker substrate will yield better flatness, but the greater the deflection when bending, the greater the stress concentration, which can be destructive to the device. ITO is rigid and brittle, and the thicker ITO will be less flexible. Zardetto et al. have conducted an in-depth study on the ultimate bending radius of flexible conductive substrates, and the results show that when the bending radius is less than 14 mm, ITO films will have micro-cracks and the conductivity will decrease rapidly.15 The micro-cracks of ITO film after bending are mainly due to the intrinsic brittleness and thickness of inorganic matter. Therefore, ITO sputtered on the flexible substrate should pay attention to the thickness when ensuring good electrical conductivity, which could ensure that the polymer foil with ITO maintain some flexibility and transparency. 2.2 Elastic flexible transparent substrates Flexible solar cells generally only need to have bendable characteristics with low strain, while stretchable solar cells require that the substrate, active layer, and transparent electrodes are all capable of generating elastic strains between 20% and 80%, and are able to return to their original state and maintain a certain level of photovoltaic performance. For stretchable solar cells, polydimethylsiloxane (PDMS) and polyurethane (PU) are the most commonly used elastic flexible transparent substrate. PDMS elastomers have been highlighted as a promising candidate for applications of more scenarios due to their low Young's modulus (∼3 MPa), high specific power, and excellent biocompatibility.11, 16, 17 Elastic flexible transparent electrode is generally made on elastomers so that flexible photovoltaic device could back to its original shape after the stretching range of Hooke's law.12, 18, 19 PDMS not only has high transparency but also can satisfy > 100% elastic deformation.12, 13 The elastic flexible transparent may be the best choice for fabricating flexible devices because it enables the device to achieve broad flexibility, such as bending, stretching, and twisting.20-23 The fabrication of flexible stretchable photovoltaic device can generally be divided into pre-stretching and post-stretching (Figure 2A,C). Pre-stretched devices require the following steps: firstly, a flexible substrate (such as PDMS) is pre-stretched and fixed on a rigid substrate; secondly, by depositing the active material on a pre-stretched substrate with wrinkles, the wrinkles are mainly created by the pre-strain method.12, 13, 18, 26 The elastic substrate is pre-stretched to a certain extent and then coated with suitable transport layers, active materials, and electrodes before release; finally, by releasing the stress, the surface area covered with the active material is reduced, resulting in a shrunken morphology.16, 25 When the device is stretched again, within the deformation range of pre-stretching, the wrinkles are elongated and flattened without destroying the internal structure of the electrode, and the device can also maintain > 50% PCE. If the tensile deformation exceeds the pre-stretching deformation, the device may crack. At this time, the modulus of the device should be considered. The elastic substrate and electrode only provide the lower limit to withstand the stretchability of the device. Chen et al. constructed a stretchable OSC using a tape with a PCE of 5.2%, a short-circuit current density (JSC) of 12.7 mA·cm−2, and an open-circuit voltage (VOC) of 0.75 V, with a fill factor (FF) of 54.4%—the highest value reported at the time.18 After 50 cycles of stretching at 20% strain, the device retained 80% of its pristine PCE. Post-stretched devices are generally fabricated on an unstretched (or relaxed) substrate, peeled from a rigid substrate, attached to an elastomer in a stretched state, and finally relieved of stress on the elastomer.11-13 A more general and efficient approach is to develop intrinsically flexible transparent electrodes (FTE) by using low-dimensional materials to build onto some polymer thin-layer substrates with rough surfaces or more wrinkles.12, 27 For example, randomly dispersed carbon nanotubes (CNTs) or Ag NWs coated on PDMS substrates. The randomly distributed CNTs or Ag NWs overlap each other to create many connection points.28-30 There will be slippage during stretching, but it will still be able to maintain most of the connection, enough to form a closed loop. There are also some CNTs or Ag NWs in the curled state that are straightened when stretched (Figure 2B). FTE can generally withstand a small stretching range but have sufficiently high electrical conductivity and light transmittance, which can also satisfy many flexible photovoltaic devices.8, 13 Whether pre-stretched or post-stretched devices, FTE is first bonded to the transparent stretchable substrate, and the wrinkled wavy structure is formed through pre-stretching or post-stretching, and finally through the rebound of the substrate. The structure could withstand mechanical strain caused by deformation (bending or stretching) while maintaining sufficient electrical conductivity, light transmission, and fatigue resistance.7, 31 Li et al. spin-coated PDMS substrates on commercial 3M sandpaper to form a textured structure on the PDMS surface and maintain the good bendability and stretchability of PDMS.32 The high diffuse transmittance and haze effect of the textured structure of the substrate leads to excellent anti-reflection properties. Devices with surface-textured electrodes exhibit a high PCE of 15.3% under 1-sun illumination (AM 1.5G), with higher JSC than their planar counterparts. Recently, Wang et al. prepared stretchable OSCs with a PCE of 16.4% on thermoplastic PU substrate, and the devices were able to maintain 80% of the initial PCE value under 80% strain.33 This is the highest PCE value and the maximum stretchability of stretchable OSCs. Another approach to stretchability is so-called “shape engineering,” extrinsic stretchable but flexible (or ultra-thin) devices.26, 34 Serpentine rigid metal NWs attached to elastic substrate could counteract mechanical failures during deformation (Figure 2D–E). In addition, thin and stiff materials can be produced on pre-stretched elastomers. The rigid material becomes a “wave” shape when the substrate subsequently relaxes back to its original shape. The curved, wavy, rigid material can change its shape when stretched externally without mechanical failure, resulting in flexible photovoltaic devices. However, this ingenious shape engineering also allows for only limited stretchability, and components beyond that can fail catastrophically, losing functionality such as electrical conductivity, semi-conductivity, and so on. Various fibers can also be woven into stretchable fabric electrodes, which can be stretched to 100−200%.21, 22, 27 For example, by dipping common fabrics into single-walled CNTs ink to fabricate transparent electrode, flexible photovoltaic device can be cycled for 100 cycles at 120% tensile deformation without significant capacitance decay. Wang et al. designed a kirigami-inspired unique island-chain structure with serpentine interconnect.26 The devices showed lower loss (87% of the initial PCE) after the stretch test. Besides the device structure, rational packaging of the device is also a reliable method to improve stretchability. Lipomi et al. significantly improved the stretchability (i.e., crack resistance under tensile deformation) of organic semiconductor films produced by encapsulation.35 Poly(3-heptylthiophene):[6,6]-phenyl-C61-butyric acid methyl ester (P3HpT:PC61BM) films on polyurethane (PU) showed 6.6 ± 0.5% cracks, showing 40 ± 4% cracks in the case of encapsulation. Conductive polymer PEDOT:PSS films also exhibit crack inhibition upon encapsulation and resistance-to-strain dependence after cracking decreased (this indicates a larger available strain range for encapsulated vs. unencapsulated films).35 In particular, good encapsulation has been shown to greatly inhibit the formation and growth of cracks in thin films of materials associated with OSCs. 3 FLEXIBLE TRANSPARENT ELECTRODES MATERIALS In common photovoltaic devices, the transparent electrodes material is generally ITO, but metal oxides have a high modulus and little flexibility.15 In addition, the selection of suitable materials to replace the traditional transparent conductive oxide films is one of the key points of research. ITO is frequently break or form cracks under small strains, even though the majority of flexible devices are currently made on ITO electrode.36-39 The following qualities are typically present in new candidates prepared for FTE: high electrical conductivity, high transparency, flexibility and elasticity, robustness, ease of manufacture, and within a specific stretch range, no change or a change of less than 10% in the resistance and transmittance of the electrode.40-43 Therefore, to evaluate tr

Title: 84a06c_290bda798b6949ab879ead6b6b23f93b.pdf URL Source: https://9021fdc6-0afa-473e-b60c-1dbccce51d7c.filesusr.com/ugd/84a06c_290bda798b6949ab879ead6b6b23f93b.pdf?index=true Published Time: Tue, 13 Oct 2020 12:18:56 GMT Number of Pages: 1 Markdown Content: Enhancement of resistance against damp h eat aging through compositional ## change in PV encapsulant poly (ethylene -co -vinyl acetat e) Abstract : The most common encapsulant used in the photovol taic (PV) modules is poly (eth ylene -co - vinyl acetate) (PEVA). The characteristics of PEVA can be varied by modulating its vinyl acetate (VA) content. According to the general consensus within the PV industry, PEVA as an encapsulant should have VA content b etween 28 and 33%. Degradation of PEVA films due to environmental stressors like humidity, temperature and solar radiation in the field aged modules is not uncommon. Moreover, degradation of PEVA encapsulan t implies a concomitant loss in the electrical per formance of the PV modules. VA content modulation especially outside the most commonly used and accepted range has been tried in this study to improve the damp heat aging resistance of PEVA films. PEVA films containing 18, 24, 33, and 40% VA content with t he necessary additives have been fabricated and cured at 150 °C under vacuum and subsequently subjected to accelerated damp -heat (85◦C and 85% RH) aging according to IEC 61215 standard for 1000 and 2000 h. Tensile strength, thermal stability and degree of crosslinking have been found to be greater for PEVA containing lower VA content (18 and 24%) and diminished for PEVA with higher VA content (33 and 40%) due to DH aging. However, inadequate % transmittance and relatively high stiffness after 2000 h of DH a ging discourages PEVA18 as an encapsulant. Moreover, PEVAs with higher VA content (33 and 40%) have been degraded significantly after 2000 h of DH aging r esulting in significant loss in transmittance and mechanical integrity. Therefore, the results of this investigation encourage the PV industry to reduce the VA content in PEVA down to 24% for better encapsulation response against DH aging.
